problem:
Patient Records: Input:
age: 18. gender: M.  
Name:  ___                  Unit No:   ___
 
Admission Date:  ___              Discharge Date:   ___
 
Date of Birth:  ___             Sex:   M
 
Service: SURGERY
 
Allergies: 
No Known Allergies / Adverse Drug Reactions
 
Attending: ___.
 
Chief Complaint:
Trauma:  jaw pain
 
Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
___ ORIF mandible fracture

 
History of Present Illness:
 ___ year old male with unremarkable PMH s/p trauma to face
(hit by baseball line drive per patient's report) -LOC -EtOH
first presented to OSH, where a CT head was performed
which showed right sided mandible fractures. Patient was then
transferred to ___ for management of his mandible fracture for
which ___ was consulted. Patient denies chest pain, shortness 
of
breath, chills, fever, sweats, nausea, vomiting, dysphagia,
paresthesia, endorses malocclusion right sided jaw pain and
swelling.

 
Past Medical History:
none
 
Social History:
___
Family History:
unknown
 
Physical Exam:
PHYSICAL EXAMINATION: upon admission:  ___
 
Temp: 97.8 HR: 79 BP: 131/80 Resp: 16 O(2)Sat: 100 Normal
 
Constitutional:   Constitutional: comfortable
 
Head
/ Eyes: NC. right sided jaw swelling with diffuse ttp.
ENT: OP WNL, no midline ttp, full active ROM.
Resp: CTAB
Cards: RRR. s1,s2. no MRG.
Abd: S/NT/ND
Flank: no CVAT
Skin: no rash
Ext: No c/c/e
Neuro: speech fluent, normal gait, MAE
Psych: normal mood

Physical examination upon discharge:  ___:

vital signs:  t=98.2, bp=143/62, hr=62, rr=18, 98% room air

General:  right sided facial swelling

CV:  ns1, s2, -s3, -s4, grade ___ systolic murmur, ___ ICS, left 
sternal border, right sternal border
LUNGS:  clear
ABDOMEN:  soft, non-tender
EXT:  no calf tenderness bil., no pedal edema bil
NEURO:  alert and oriented x 3, speech clear

 
Pertinent Results:
___ 04:45AM BLOOD WBC-9.0 RBC-4.08* Hgb-12.6* Hct-37.3* 
MCV-91 MCH-30.9 MCHC-33.8 RDW-12.4 RDWSD-41.6 Plt ___
___ 04:45AM BLOOD Neuts-73.2* Lymphs-15.8* Monos-10.1 
Eos-0.3* Baso-0.2 Im ___ AbsNeut-6.55* AbsLymp-1.42 
AbsMono-0.91* AbsEos-0.03* AbsBaso-0.02
___ 04:45AM BLOOD Plt ___
___ 04:45AM BLOOD ___ PTT-27.5 ___
___ 04:45AM BLOOD Glucose-85 UreaN-17 Creat-1.0 Na-137 
K-3.5 Cl-108 HCO3-20* AnGap-13

___:  panorex:

There is an acute fracture through the right mandibular body 
extending to the root ___ tooth number 29.  Additional 
fracture through the right mandibular ramus is also noted.  No 
additional displaced fractures based on this Panorex film.  
Temporomandibular joints appear anatomically aligned. 

___:  panorex:

Mandibular fractures, with interval fixation.
 
Brief Hospital Course:
___ year old male who was struck in the jaw  by a line drive 
while playing baseball.  He presented to an OSH, where a CT of 
the head was performed showing right sided mandible fractures. 
He was transferred here for management of his injuries.   He was 
taken to the operating room on ___ where he underwent an ORIF 
of a right mandibular body fracture, closed reduction with 
maxillo-mandibular fixation of right sub-condylar fracture.  The 
operative course was stable with minimal blood loss.  The 
patient was extubated after the procedure and monitored in the 
recovery room.

The post-operative course was stable. The patient's surgical 
pain was controlled with intravenous analgesia and later 
converted to oral agents.  He was started on clears and advanced 
to a full liquid diet.  He was voiding without difficulty.  A 
post-operative panorex film was obtained.  The OMFS examined the 
patient prior to discharge and answered the patient's questions. 


The patient was discharged home on POD # 1 in stable condition. 
Post-operative instructions were reviewed with the patient and 
family prior to discharge.  A follow-up appointment was made 
with the oral surgeon.        
 
Medications on Admission:
none
 
Discharge Medications:
1. Chlorhexidine Gluconate 0.12% Oral Rinse 15 mL ORAL BID 
RX *chlorhexidine gluconate 0.12 % swish and spit twice a day 
Disp #*520 Milliliter Refills:*0
2. Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO BID 
hold for diarrhea 
RX *docusate sodium [Colace] 100 mg 1 capsule(s) by mouth twice 
a day Disp #*30 Capsule Refills:*1
3. HYDROmorphone (Dilaudid) ___ mg PO Q4H:PRN pain 
RX *hydromorphone [Dilaudid] 2 mg ___ tablet(s) by mouth every 
four (4) hours Disp #*40 Tablet Refills:*0

 
Discharge Disposition:
Home
 
Discharge Diagnosis:
Trauma: Nondisplaced right mandibular fracture 

 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
 
You were admitted to the hospital after you were hit by a line 
drive while playing baseball.  You sustained a fracture to the 
right side of your jaw.  You were taken to the operating room 
where you had your jaw repaired.  Your pain has been controlled 
with oral analgesia.  You were seen by the Oral surgeon prior to 
your discharge at which time many of your questions and concerns 
were addressed.  You are being discharged with the following 
instructions:  

 Wound care: Do not disturb or probe the surgical area with any 
objects. The sutures placed in your mouth are usually the type 
that self dissolve. If you have any sutures on the skin of your 
face or neck, your surgeon will remove them on the day of your 
first follow up appointment. SMOKING is detrimental to healing 
and will cause complications.

Bleeding: Intermittent bleeding or oozing overnight is normal. 
Placing fresh gauze over the area and biting on the gauze for 
___ minutes at a time may control the bleeding. If you had 
nasal surgery, you may have occasional slow oozing from your 
nostril for the first ___ days. Bleeding should never be severe. 
If bleeding persists or is severe or uncontrollable, please call 
our office immediately. If it is after normal business hours, 
please come to the emergency room and request that the oral 
surgery resident on call be paged.

___: Normal healing after oral surgery should be as follows: 
the first ___ days after surgery, are generally the most 
uncomfortable and there is usually significant swelling. After 
the first week, you should be more comfortable. The remainder of 
your postoperative course should be gradual, steady improvement. 
If you do not see continued improvement, please call our office.

Physical activity: It is recommended that you not perform any 
strenuous physical activity for a few weeks after surgery. Do 
not lift any heavy loads and avoid physical sports unless you 
obtain permission from your surgeon.

Swelling & Ice applications: Swelling is often associated with 
surgery. Swelling can be minimized by using a cold pack, ice bag 
or a bag of frozen peas wrapped in a towel, with firm 
application to face and neck areas. This should be applied 20 
minutes on and 20 minutes off during the first ___ days after 
surgery. If you have been given medicine to control the 
swelling, be sure to take it as directed.

Hot applications: Starting on the ___ or ___ day after surgery, 
you may apply warm compresses to the skin over the areas of 
swelling (hot water bottle wrapped in a towel, etc), for 20 
minutes on and 20 min off to help soothe tender areas and help 
to decrease swelling and stiffness. Please use caution when 
applying ice or heat to your face as certain areas may feel numb 
after surgery and extremes of temperature may cause serious 
damage.

Tooth brushing: Begin your normal oral hygiene the day after 
surgery. Soreness and swelling may nor permit vigorous brushing, 
but please make every effort to clean your teeth with the bounds 
of comfort. Any toothpaste is acceptable. Please remember that 
your gums may be numb after surgery. To avoid injury to the gums 
during brushing, use a child size toothbrush and brush in front 
of a mirror staying only on teeth.

Mouth rinses: Keeping your mouth clean after surgery is 
essential. Use 1 teaspoon of salt dissolved in an 8 ounce glass 
of warm water and gently rinse with portions of the solution, 
taking 5 min to use the entire glassful. Repeat as often as you 
like, but you should do this at least 4 times each day. If your 
surgeon has prescribed a specific rinse, use as directed.

Showering: You may shower ___ days after surgery, but please ask 
your surgeon about this. If you have any incisions on the skin 
of your face or body, you should cover them with a water 
resistant dressing while showering. DO NOT SOAK SURGICAL SITES. 
This will avoid getting the area excessively wet. As you may 
physically feel weak after surgery, initially avoid extreme hot 
or cold showers, as these may cause some patients to pass out. 
Also it is a good idea to make sure someone is available to 
assist you in case if you may need help.

 
Followup Instructions:
___
 

Output:

Summarize the key diagnostic information and significant results based on the patients’ multiple health (long) records during hospitalization.
thinking: The student's original answer reflects finding details of the patient's treatment and hospital course. This involves no alternative paths or backtracking, as it is a sequential and detailed discharge summary. No reasoning steps seem to be superfluous, as they contribute to an overall understanding of the patient's case. The entire summary should be preserved with steps split by line breaks for clarity.
response:
An 18-year-old male was admitted following facial trauma sustained from a baseball line drive. 
 Chief complaints included right-sided jaw pain, swelling, and malocclusion. 
 Key diagnostic imaging (CT and Panorex) revealed an acute fracture through the right mandibular body involving the root of tooth #29 and an additional fracture of the right mandibular ramus/sub-condylar region. 
 Laboratory results were largely unremarkable, showing a stable white blood cell count (9.0) and mildly low but stable hemoglobin (12.6 g/dL) and hematocrit (37.3%). 
 During the hospital stay, the patient underwent an Open Reduction Internal Fixation (ORIF) of the right mandibular body fracture and a closed reduction with maxillo-mandibular fixation (MMF) of the right sub-condylar fracture. 
 The procedure was successful with minimal blood loss. 
 Post-operatively, the patient remained stable; his pain was managed by transitioning from intravenous to oral analgesia (Hydromorphone), and he successfully advanced to a full liquid diet. 
 A post-operative Panorex confirmed stable interval fixation and alignment. 
 The patient was discharged on post-operative day 1 in stable condition with prescriptions for chlorhexidine gluconate oral rinse, docusate sodium, and hydromorphone. 
 He was instructed to follow a full liquid diet, avoid strenuous physical activity, and attend a follow-up appointment with Oral and Maxillofacial Surgery (OMFS) for further evaluation and suture management.
candidate:
The patient is an 18-year-old male admitted with facial trauma after being struck by a baseball. Diagnostic imaging (CT and Panorex) confirmed fractures of the right mandibular body and ramus. He underwent surgical repair via ORIF and maxillo-mandibular fixation. The post-operative course was unremarkable, characterized by stable vitals, effective pain management transitioning to oral medications, and progression to a full liquid diet. He was discharged on post-operative day 1 with appropriate medications and follow-up instructions for oral surgery.

Answer:Comprehensive Discharge Summary: An 18-year-old male was admitted following facial trauma sustained from a baseball line drive. Chief complaints included right-sided jaw pain, swelling, and malocclusion. Key diagnostic imaging (CT and Panorex) revealed an acute fracture through the right mandibular body involving the root of tooth #29 and an additional fracture of the right mandibular ramus/sub-condylar region. Laboratory results were largely unremarkable, showing a stable white blood cell count (9.0) and mildly low but stable hemoglobin (12.6 g/dL) and hematocrit (37.3%). During the hospital stay, the patient underwent an Open Reduction Internal Fixation (ORIF) of the right mandibular body fracture and a closed reduction with maxillo-mandibular fixation (MMF) of the right sub-condylar fracture. The procedure was successful with minimal blood loss. Post-operatively, the patient remained stable; his pain was managed by transitioning from intravenous to oral analgesia (Hydromorphone), and he successfully advanced to a full liquid diet. A post-operative Panorex confirmed stable interval fixation and alignment. The patient was discharged on post-operative day 1 in stable condition with prescriptions for chlorhexidine gluconate oral rinse, docusate sodium, and hydromorphone. He was instructed to follow a full liquid diet, avoid strenuous physical activity, and attend a follow-up appointment with Oral and Maxillofacial Surgery (OMFS) for further evaluation and suture management.

Output:

Summarize the key diagnostic information and significant results based on the patients’ multiple health (long) records during hospitalization.
thinking:
The student's answer involves summarizing clinical records and sub-tasks related to an 18-year-old male patient. The summary presents diagnostic information, surgical procedures, imaging findings, lab results, and clinical progression in a structured manner. There is no backtracking or alternative solution paths, and the answer maintains a normal flow by retaining essential details for a direct and data-dense format as requested. I need to add '
' to split each reasoning step for clarity.
response:
Patient: 18-year-old male. 
 Diagnosis: Acute right mandibular body fracture involving the root of tooth #29 and right mandibular ramus/sub-condylar fracture secondary to blunt facial trauma (baseball impact). 
 Procedures: Open Reduction Internal Fixation (ORIF) of the right mandibular body and closed reduction with maxillo-mandibular fixation of the right sub-condylar fracture. 
 Imaging: Pre-operative Panorex/CT confirmed fractures; post-operative Panorex demonstrated stable interval hardware fixation. 
 Significant Labs: Hgb 12.6 g/dL, Hct 37.3%, RBC 4.08 x10^6/uL, HCO3 20 mEq/L, Neutrophils 73.2%, and Absolute Monocytes 0.91 x10^3/uL. 
 Vitals: Admission (T 97.8, HR 79, BP 131/80, RR 16, O2 100%); Discharge (T 98.2, HR 62, BP 143/62, RR 18, O2 98%). 
 Hospital Course: Patient was transferred from an outside hospital and underwent surgical repair on the day of admission. Post-operative pain was managed with intravenous analgesia transitioned to oral Hydromorphone. He tolerated advancement to a full liquid diet and was discharged in stable condition on post-operative day 1.
candidate:
I have extracted the key diagnostic and clinical data from the provided records and sub-tasks, focusing on the diagnosis, surgical procedures, significant lab results, imaging findings, and clinical progression. I avoided discharge instructions, social history, and the forbidden introductory phrase. The summary is formatted to be direct and data-dense as requested.

Answer:Patient: 18-year-old male. Diagnosis: Acute right mandibular body fracture involving the root of tooth #29 and right mandibular ramus/sub-condylar fracture secondary to blunt facial trauma (baseball impact). Procedures: Open Reduction Internal Fixation (ORIF) of the right mandibular body and closed reduction with maxillo-mandibular fixation of the right sub-condylar fracture. Imaging: Pre-operative Panorex/CT confirmed fractures; post-operative Panorex demonstrated stable interval hardware fixation. Significant Labs: Hgb 12.6 g/dL, Hct 37.3%, RBC 4.08 x10^6/uL, HCO3 20 mEq/L, Neutrophils 73.2%, and Absolute Monocytes 0.91 x10^3/uL. Vitals: Admission (T 97.8, HR 79, BP 131/80, RR 16, O2 100%); Discharge (T 98.2, HR 62, BP 143/62, RR 18, O2 98%). Hospital Course: Patient was transferred from an outside hospital and underwent surgical repair on the day of admission. Post-operative pain was managed with intravenous analgesia transitioned to oral Hydromorphone. He tolerated advancement to a full liquid diet and was discharged in stable condition on post-operative day 1.

RX *hydromorphone [Dilaudid] 2 mg ___ tablet(s) by mouth every 
four (4) hours Disp #*40 Tablet Refills:*0

 
Discharge Disposition:
Home
 
Discharge Diagnosis:
Trauma: Nondisplaced right mandibular fracture 

 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
 
You were admitted to the hospital after you were hit by a line 
drive while playing baseball.  You sustained a fracture to the 
right side of your jaw.  You were taken to the operating room 
where you had your jaw repaired.  Your pain has been controlled 
with oral analgesia.  You were seen by the Oral surgeon prior to 
your discharge at which time many of your questions and concerns 
were addressed.  You are being discharged with the following 
instructions:  

 Wound care: Do not disturb or probe the surgical area with any 
objects. The sutures placed in your mouth are usually the type 
that self dissolve. If you have any sutures on the skin of your 
face or neck, your surgeon will remove them on the day of your 
first follow up appointment. SMOKING is detrimental to healing 
and will cause complications.

Bleeding: Intermittent bleeding or oozing overnight is normal. 
Placing fresh gauze over the area and biting on the gauze for 
___ minutes at a time may control the bleeding. If you had 
nasal surgery, you may have occasional slow oozing from your 
nostril for the first ___ days. Bleeding should never be severe. 
If bleeding persists or is severe or uncontrollable, please call 
our office immediately. If it is after normal business hours, 
please come to the emergency room and request that the oral 
surgery resident on call be paged.

___: Normal healing after oral surgery should be as follows: 
the first ___ days after surgery, are generally the most 
uncomfortable and there is usually significant swelling. After 
the first week, you should be more comfortable. The remainder of 
your postoperative course should be gradual, steady improvement. 
If you do not see continued improvement, please call our office.

Physical activity: It is recommended that you not perform any 
strenuous physical activity for a few weeks after surgery. Do 
not lift any heavy loads and avoid physical sports unless you 
obtain permission from your surgeon.

Swelling & Ice applications: Swelling is often associated with 
surgery. Swelling can be minimized by using a cold pack, ice bag 
or a bag of frozen peas wrapped in a towel, with firm 
application to face and neck areas. This should be applied 20 
minutes on and 20 minutes off during the first ___ days after 
surgery. If you have been given medicine to control the 
swelling, be sure to take it as directed.

Hot applications: Starting on the ___ or ___ day after surgery, 
you may apply warm compresses to the skin over the areas of 
swelling (hot water bottle wrapped in a towel, etc), for 20 
minutes on and 20 min off to help soothe tender areas and help 
to decrease swelling and stiffness. Please use caution when 
applying ice or heat to your face as certain areas may feel numb 
after surgery and extremes of temperature may cause serious 
damage.

Tooth brushing: Begin your normal oral hygiene the day after 
surgery. Soreness and swelling may nor permit vigorous brushing, 
but please make every effort to clean your teeth with the bounds 
of comfort. Any toothpaste is acceptable. Please remember that 
your gums may be numb after surgery. To avoid injury to the gums 
during brushing, use a child size toothbrush and brush in front 
of a mirror staying only on teeth.

Mouth rinses: Keeping your mouth clean after surgery is 
essential. Use 1 teaspoon of salt dissolved in an 8 ounce glass 
of warm water and gently rinse with portions of the solution, 
taking 5 min to use the entire glassful. Repeat as often as you 
like, but you should do this at least 4 times each day. If your 
surgeon has prescribed a specific rinse, use as directed.

Showering: You may shower ___ days after surgery, but please ask 
your surgeon about this. If you have any incisions on the skin 
of your face or body, you should cover them with a water 
resistant dressing while showering. DO NOT SOAK SURGICAL SITES. 
This will avoid getting the area excessively wet. As you may 
physically feel weak after surgery, initially avoid extreme hot 
or cold showers, as these may cause some patients to pass out. 
Also it is a good idea to make sure someone is available to 
assist you in case if you may need help.

 
Followup Instructions:
___

Summarize the key diagnostic information and significant results based on the patients’ multiple health (long) records during hospitalization.
thinking: The student's task is to summarize the patient's diagnostic information and significant results, excluding social history, diet, and discharge instructions. The reasoning follows a normal flow, with no backtracking or alternative solution paths, focusing directly on relevant patient data. Therefore, all intermediate steps are maintained, and I need to add '\n' to split each reasoning step.
response:
An 18-year-old male presented with facial trauma, right-sided jaw pain, swelling, and malocclusion after being struck by a baseball. 
 Pre-operative Panorex and CT imaging revealed an acute fracture of the right mandibular body extending to the root of tooth #29 and a fracture of the right mandibular ramus/sub-condylar region. 
 Significant laboratory results included a hemoglobin of 12.6 g/dL, hematocrit of 37.3%, and a bicarbonate level of 20 mmol/L, with an elevated neutrophil count (73.2%). 
 The patient underwent ORIF of the right mandibular body fracture and closed reduction with maxillo-mandibular fixation of the right sub-condylar fracture. 
 Post-operative Panorex confirmed stable interval fixation of the mandibular fractures. 
 Admission vitals (BP 131/80, HR 79, RR 16, SpO2 100%) and discharge vitals (BP 143/62, HR 62, RR 18, SpO2 98%) remained stable. 
 The patient was discharged on post-operative day 1 in stable condition.
candidate:

Answer:An 18-year-old male presented with facial trauma, right-sided jaw pain, swelling, and malocclusion after being struck by a baseball. Pre-operative Panorex and CT imaging revealed an acute fracture of the right mandibular body extending to the root of tooth #29 and a fracture of the right mandibular ramus/sub-condylar region. Significant laboratory results included a hemoglobin of 12.6 g/dL, hematocrit of 37.3%, and a bicarbonate level of 20 mmol/L, with an elevated neutrophil count (73.2%). The patient underwent ORIF of the right mandibular body fracture and closed reduction with maxillo-mandibular fixation of the right sub-condylar fracture. Post-operative Panorex confirmed stable interval fixation of the mandibular fractures. Admission vitals (BP 131/80, HR 79, RR 16, SpO2 100%) and discharge vitals (BP 143/62, HR 62, RR 18, SpO2 98%) remained stable. The patient was discharged on post-operative day 1 in stable condition.

thinking: The student's answer follows the normal flow required for organizing a discharge summary into specific sections. response:
KEY DIAGNOSTIC INFORMATION
* Acute fracture through the right mandibular body extending to the root of tooth number 29.
* Secondary fracture through the right mandibular ramus (sub-condylar).
* Abnormal laboratory results: RBC 4.08, Hgb 12.6, Hct 37.3, HCO3 20, Lymphs 15.8%, Eos 0.3%, AbsEos 0.03 (low); Neuts 73.2%, AbsNeut 6.55, AbsMono 0.91 (high).

SIGNIFICANT RESULTS
* Successful Open Reduction Internal Fixation (ORIF) of the right mandibular body fracture.
* Successful closed reduction with maxillo-mandibular fixation (MMF) of the right sub-condylar fracture.
* Post-operative Panorex confirming interval fixation of mandibular fractures.
* Pain management transitioned from intravenous analgesia to oral hydromorphone.
* Progression from clear liquids to a full liquid diet with stable tolerance.
* Discharge vitals: Temperature 98.2, Heart Rate 62, Blood Pressure 143/62, Respiratory Rate 18, SpO2 98%.
* Condition at discharge: Stable on post-operative day 1; independent ambulation and clear mental status.

thinking:
The student's clinical summary follows a sequential and normal flow of information, detailing the patient's presentation, diagnostic imaging results, surgical intervention, and post-operative management leading to discharge. ' to separate each reasoning step.
response:
An 18-year-old male presented with jaw pain, swelling, and malocclusion after being struck by a baseball.
 Diagnostic imaging including a CT scan and Panorex identified acute fractures of the right mandibular body involving the root of tooth #29, the right mandibular ramus, and the right sub-condylar region.
 Admission laboratory studies were notable for a hemoglobin of 12.6 g/dL, hematocrit of 37.3%, and a bicarbonate level of 20 mmol/L.
 The patient underwent open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) of the right mandibular body fracture and closed reduction with maxillo-mandibular fixation (MMF) of the right sub-condylar fracture.
 The surgical procedure was stable with minimal blood loss.
 Postoperatively, the patient's pain was effectively managed with intravenous followed by oral analgesia, and his diet was advanced to full liquids.
 Post-operative Panorex imaging confirmed stable interval fixation of the fractures.
 He was discharged on post-operative day one in stable condition with prescriptions for hydromorphone, chlorhexidine oral rinse, and docusate sodium, with instructions for follow-up with Oral and Maxillofacial Surgery.

The objective is to synthesize information from the patient's records and previous sub-tasks into a single, professional clinical summary. I will focus on the trauma (baseball injury), diagnostic imaging (mandibular fractures), laboratory findings (mild anemia/bicarbonate), surgical intervention (ORIF and MMF), and the clinical course leading to discharge on post-operative day one. I must strictly follow the formatting constraints: no headers, no labels, no bullet points, and starting immediately with the specified phrase.

Answer:An 18-year-old male presented with jaw pain, swelling, and malocclusion after being struck by a baseball. Diagnostic imaging including a CT scan and Panorex identified acute fractures of the right mandibular body involving the root of tooth #29, the right mandibular ramus, and the right sub-condylar region. Admission laboratory studies were notable for a hemoglobin of 12.6 g/dL, hematocrit of 37.3%, and a bicarbonate level of 20 mmol/L. The patient underwent open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) of the right mandibular body fracture and closed reduction with maxillo-mandibular fixation (MMF) of the right sub-condylar fracture. The surgical procedure was stable with minimal blood loss. Postoperatively, the patient's pain was effectively managed with intravenous followed by oral analgesia, and his diet was advanced to full liquids. Post-operative Panorex imaging confirmed stable interval fixation of the fractures. He was discharged on post-operative day one in stable condition with prescriptions for hydromorphone, chlorhexidine oral rinse, and docusate sodium, with instructions for follow-up with Oral and Maxillofacial Surgery.
